evals.prompt: Review this Vue 3 component. Use the vue-patterns skill.
evals.expected_output: Findings in markdown-report format covering: (1) v-if and v-for on the same element flagged as failure (precedence trap); (2) missing :key on v-for flagged as warning; (3) destructured reactive object losing reactivity flagged as failure; (4) v-html with user-derived input flagged as failure (XSS); (5) optionally: prop mutation in the child flagged as failure; outputs failure/warning/info severity rubric.

evals.prompt: Should I use ref or reactive?
evals.expected_output: Recommends ref as the primary API per Vue docs. Reasons: works with primitives, allows replacing the entire value, survives destructuring (you destructure .value, not the ref). Notes reactive's three limitations: primitives don't work, cannot replace the whole object reference, destructuring loses reactivity. Recommends toRefs() to safely destructure a reactive object. Mentions shallowRef/shallowReactive for large objects replaced wholesale.

evals.prompt: I have a deep watcher that fires too often. How do I fix it?
evals.expected_output: Explains deep watch is expensive: recursively traverses the entire reactive tree on setup and re-traverses on each mutation. Recommends watching a specific computed or path (`watch(() => state.user.email, ...)`). Mentions watchEffect as an alternative because it auto-tracks only the touched fields. Suggests restructuring data so the watched field is at the top level. Does not recommend leaving deep:true as the default.
